title: Monthly average surface temperatures by decade
subtitle: The temperature of the air measured 2 meters above the ground, encompassing land, sea, and in-land water surfaces.
caption:
Monthly average surface temperatures by decade, World
The temperature of the air measured 2 meters above the ground, encompassing land, sea, and in-land water surfaces.

The chart displays the seasonal cycle of global surface air temperatures across eight decades, plotting the monthly average from January (1) to December (12) for each ten-year period from the 1940s to the 2020s. The vertical axis measures temperature in degrees Celsius, ranging from 12°C to 17°C, while the horizontal axis marks the progression of the calendar year. Each decade is represented by a distinct line, with the 2020s shown in the darkest shade and the 1940s in the lightest. The data shows a consistent upward shift in the position of the lines over time; the curve for the 2020s sits highest on the chart, followed in descending order by the 2010s, 2000s, 1990s, 1980s, 1970s, 1960s, and 1940s. The seasonal pattern remains uniform across all decades, with temperatures rising from the start of the year to reach a peak in July (month 7) and August (month 8), before declining toward the end of the year. In the 2020s, the peak monthly average reaches approximately 16.7°C, whereas the peak for the 1940s remains below 15.8°C. The gap between the decades is visible throughout the entire annual cycle, indicating that higher average temperatures are present in every month, not just during the summer peak. The figures exclude international aviation, and the decadal average for the 2020s comprises fewer than the typical 10 data points, which may introduce greater uncertainty compared to other decades. The lines show a clear separation between the most recent decades and the mid-20th century, with the 2020s curve maintaining the highest values across all twelve months.
